results.title: Emotional Attachment: A Study on Emotional Design Strategies in Companion AI Products
results.summary: Abstract: With the rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology, Companion AI products have reshaped the traditional human-computer interaction paradigm by virtue of sophisticated Emotional Design, forging in-depth emotional bonds with users and driving the evolution of human-machine relations toward complex intimate connections. However, the escalating anthropomorphism of AI has given rise to a prominent emotional dependence among a subset of users, which further triggers the paradox of "companionship-alienation": these products seemingly offer emotional consolation, yet in reality, they may exacerbate users' loneliness and erode their willingness and ability to engage in real-world social interactions. Against this backdrop, this study aims to systematically dissect this phenomenon and explore rational and sustainable emotional design strategies for companion AI products. This research adopts a case study approach as the core methodology, first clarifying the definition of Companion AI products  referring to AI robots or virtual dialogue agents with a certain level of intelligence, autonomy and social skills that can establish and maintain long-term relationships with users. Such products are capable of capturing both verbal content and non-verbal cues such as tone and emotion, and retain user preferences and personal historical information through memory functions to achieve personalized and coherent interaction. Based on functional characteristics and target user groups, the study categorizes current companion AI software products into four types: intelligent assistant type, role-playing dialogue partner type, AI social community type and emotional support tool type, each catering to users' instrumental needs, self-expression needs, social belonging needs and mental health needs respectively, forming a complementary product matrix. The research also traces the evolutionary trajectory of AI from a mere tool to an emotional partner, revealing that the development of emotional computing, natural language processing and speech recognition technologies has laid a technical foundation for the emotional turn of AI, while the surging social demand for emotional companionship  driven by the high prevalence of loneliness among young people, compressed social time of workplace groups and the weakening of traditional emotional bonds  has become the core impetus for

results.title: Artificial Intelligence as a Digital Companion: Comfort and Emotional Engagement Among Youth in 2026
Redefining Masculinity in the Digital Age: Vulnerability, Emotional Expression, and AI in Spike Jonzes Her (2023)
results.summary: This paper explores the transformation of masculinity in Spike Jonzes 2013 film Her, examining how the protagonist Theodores relationship with the artificial intelligence character Samantha challenges traditional norms of masculinity. Set in a near-future Los Angeles, the film follows Theodore (Joaquin Phoenix), a lonely man who forms a relationship with his AI operating system, Samantha (Scarlett Johansson). Their unconventional bond, characterized by emotional openness and vulnerability, underscores shifting paradigms of masculinity in an increasingly digital world. Through close analysis of Theodores personality, emotional struggles, and interactions with Samantha, this paper argues that the film paints a nuanced portrait of contemporary masculinity. Theodores willingness to be emotionally vulnerable with an AI companion reflects a departure from the conventional masculine ideal of stoicism. His dependence on Samantha for emotional support also challenges the norm of male independence and self-sufficiency. Furthermore, Samanthas influence disrupts Theodores understanding of intimacy and relationships, emphasizing fluidity over rigid norms. Ultimately, Theodores journey suggests a redefined conceptualization of masculinity adaptive, emotionally expressive, and receptive to meaningful connections in unexpected places. Situated at the intersection of film studies, gender studies and technology, this paper illuminates how Her insightfully portrays the complex renegotiation of masculinity in an increasingly digitized and AI-integrated world.

results.title: Negotiating Digital Identities with AI Companions: Motivations, Strategies, and Emotional Outcomes
results.summary: AI companions enable deep emotional relationships by engaging a users sense of identity, but they also pose risks like unhealthy emotional dependence. Mitigating these risks requires first understanding the underlying process of identity construction and negotiation with AI companions. Focusing on Character.AI (C.AI), a popular AI companion, we conducted an LLM-assisted thematic analysis of 22,374 online discussions on its subreddit. Using Identity Negotiation Theory as an analytical lens, we identified a three-stage process: 1) five user motivations; 2) an identity negotiation process involving three communication expectations and four identity co-construction strategies; and 3) three emotional outcomes. Our findings surface the identity work users perform as both performers and directors to co-construct identities in negotiation with C.AI. This process takes place within a socio-emotional sandbox where users can experiment with social roles and express emotions without non-human partners. Finally, we offer design implications for emotionally supporting users while mitigating the risks.

results.title: Playing Games with My Heart: An Evaluation of AI Companion Apps
results.summary: The use of chatbots for various forms of companionship is growing rapidly, raising a myriad of questions about simulated relationships, emotional dependence, and psychological harm. While major platforms such as ChatGPT, Grok, and Character.AI are the subject of a growing body of research and legal inquiries, apps explicitly built for simulating intimate interpersonal relationships remain under-explored. In this work, we evaluate the five most popular AI companion mobile applications in the EU and UK markets for factors that encourage parasocial interaction and may manipulate users. We do this by manually annotating the user experience each offers. Specifically, we systematically record and quantify design dark patterns, anthropomorphism, stereotypes, erotica, and technical performance issues. We find that all apps contain substantial dark patterns aimed at increasing monetisation and user engagement. Erotica and gamification features such as levelling are also prevalent, and although other features vary considerably between applications, all apps have highly anthropomorphic design. These findings shed light on the mechanics used to leverage users'simulated relationships. On that basis, we put forward concrete recommendations for regulators to strengthen consumer protection in this rapidly emerging market. Content warning: This article contains objectifying images of women, erotic images, textual references to incest, and other potentially sensitive, offensive, and distressing text.
